讲义目录: ds4-tutorial/
模式: full
当前 HEAD: 80ebbc396aee40eedc1d829222f3362d10fa4c6c
代码永久链接 base: https://github.com/antirez/ds4/blob/80ebbc396aee40eedc1d829222f3362d10fa4c6c/
用户关注点: （无）


---

## 执行要求

请按照 planner prompt 中的规则执行。

你需要输出：

- 一个完整 manifest JSON。
- full 模式下，所有讲义 `action` 为 `"new"`。
- incremental 模式下，根据 diff 判断每篇讲义的 `action`。

不要写任何 Markdown 讲义文件。

不要输出 JSON 以外的任何文字。

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# DwarfStar
2	
3	**DwarfStar** is a small native inference engine optimized first for
4	**DeepSeek V4 Flash**, with support for **DeepSeek V4 PRO** on very high-memory
5	machines. It is
6	intentionally narrow: not a generic GGUF runner, not a wrapper around another
7	runtime: it is completely self-contained. Other than running the model in a
8	correct and fast way, the project goal is to provide DeepSeek specific loading,
9	prompt rendering, tool calling, KV state handling (RAM and on-disk), server
10	API and integrated coding agent, all ready to work with coding agents or with
11	the provided CLI interface. There are also tools for GGUF and imatrix generation,
12	and for quality and speed testing.
13	
14	We support the following backends:
15	* **Metal** is our primary target. Starting from MacBooks with 96GB of RAM (or less, using SSD streaming).
16	* **NVIDIA CUDA / DGX Spark**, CUDA with special care for the DGX Spark.
17	* **Strix Halo (ROCm)**, systems like the Framework Desktop and other systems based on the same GPU and unified RAM design.
18	
19	This project would not exist without **llama.cpp and GGML**, make sure to read
20	the acknowledgements section, a big thank you to Georgi Gerganov and all the
21	other contributors.
22	
23	**Note that DeepSeek v4** is not our only target. Right now Flash and PRO are the
24	perfect fit because of capabilities, size, KV cache efficiency. If tomorrow a
25	better open weight model is released for the 128GB size, we could switch, the same
26	for other important size classes like 512GB of RAM. The project is stictly
27	opportunistic depending on what open weight models exist in a given moment.
28	If a new model will be supported, the old one may be removed completely and
29	no longer supported, unless there is some kind of overlap of abilities.
30	
31	## Motivations
32	
33	* Very capable open weight models finally exist. DeepSeek v4 Flash feels quasi-frontier. The PRO is even better. Both resist 2 bit quantization very well.
34	* Very capable computers like MacBooks, the DGX Spark now exist.
35	* DeepSeek v4 kv cache design makes it pratical to run very big contexts. Other vendors are using this approach.
36	* This few hundred billions models are strictly better than smaller (even if dense) models, regardless of what benchmarks say.
37	
38	That said, a few important things about this project:
39	
40	* The local inference landscape contains many excellent projects, but new models are released continuously, and the attention immediately gets captured by the next model to implement. This project takes a deliberately narrow bet: one model at a time, official-vector validation (logits obtained with the official implementation), long-context tests, and enough agent integration to know if it really works. The exact model may change as the landscape evolves, but the constraint remains: local inference credible on high end personal machines or Mac Studios, starting from 96/128GB of memory.
41	* This software is developed with **strong assistance from GPT 5.5** and with humans leading the ideas, testing, and debugging. We say this openly because it shaped how the project was built. If you are not happy with AI-developed code, this software is not for you. The acknowledgement below is equally important: this would not exist without `llama.cpp` and GGML, largely written by hand.
42	* This implementation is based on the idea that compressed KV caches like the one of DeepSeek v4 and the fast SSD disks of modern MacBooks should change our idea that KV cache belongs to RAM. **The KV cache is actually a first-class disk citizen**. Fast SSD disks also changed the inference game from the point of view of "model needs to fit RAM": while having more RAM the the model size is still preferred, SSD streaming allows to turn the available amount of RAM from a hard cutoff (can I run this model or not?) to continuous spectrum of speed levels.
43	* Our vision is that local inference should be a set of three things working well together, out of the box: A) inference engine with HTTP API + B) GGUF specially crafted to run well under a given engine and given assumptions + C) testing and validation with coding agents implementations. D) Purpose built agents for specific models and execution environments. DwarfStar only runs with the GGUF files provided. It gets tested against officially obtained logits at different context sizes. This project exists because we wanted to make one local model feel finished end to end, not just runnable. However this is beta quality code, so probably we are not still there, especially since recently we introduced large new features: distributed inference, SSD streaming, and other minor improvements.
44	* The optimized graph path targets **Metal on macOS** and **CUDA on Linux**. The CPU path is only for correctness checks and model/tokenizer diagnostics. For CPU-only Linux builds, use `make cpu`; it builds the normal `./ds4` and `./ds4-server` binaries without CUDA or Metal. On macOS, **warning: current macOS versions have a bug in the virtual memory implementation that will crash the kernel** if you try to run the CPU code. Remember? Software sucks. It was not possible to fix the CPU inference to avoid crashing, since each time you have to restart the computer, which is not funny. Help us, if you have the guts.
45	
46	## Acknowledgements to llama.cpp and GGML
47	
48	`ds4.c` does not link against GGML, but it **exists thanks to the path opened by the
49	llama.cpp project and the kernels, quantization formats, GGUF ecosystem, and hard-won
50	engineering knowledge developed there**.
51	We are thankful and indebted to [`llama.cpp`](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp)
52	and its contributors. Their implementation, kernels, tests, and design choices were
53	an essential reference while building this DeepSeek V4 specific inference path.
54	Some source-level pieces are retained or adapted here under the MIT license: GGUF
55	quant layouts and tables, CPU quant/dot logic, and certain kernels. For this
56	reason, and because we are genuinely grateful, we keep the GGML authors copyright
57	notice in our `LICENSE` file.
58	
59	## Status
60	
61	The code and GGUF files are to be considered of **beta quality** because
62	inference and model serving is a complicated matter and all this exists
63	only for a few days. It will take months to reach a more stable form.
64	However, we try to keep the project in a usable state, and we are making
65	progress. If you have issues, make sure to use `--trace` to log the
66	sessions, and open issues including the full trace.
67	
68	The `ds4-agent` is alpha quality, the project was later added.
69	
70	## More Documentation
71	
72	If you are looking for very specific things, we have other
73	sub-README files. Otherwise for normal usage keep reading the
74	next sections.
75	
76	- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md): correctness and speed regression testing
77	  guide for contributors. **Read this before sending a pull request**.
78	- [gguf-tools/README.md](gguf-tools/README.md): offline GGUF generation,
79	  imatrix collection, quantization tooling, and quality checks.
80	- [gguf-tools/imatrix/README.md](gguf-tools/imatrix/README.md): how the
81	  routed-MoE imatrix is collected and used.
82	- [gguf-tools/imatrix/dataset/README.md](gguf-tools/imatrix/dataset/README.md):
83	  how the calibration prompt corpus is generated.
84	- [gguf-tools/quality-testing/README.md](gguf-tools/quality-testing/README.md):
85	  how local GGUFs are scored against official DeepSeek V4 Flash/PRO continuations.
86	- [dir-steering/README.md](dir-steering/README.md): directional steering data,
87	  vector generation, and usage.
88	- [speed-bench/README.md](speed-bench/README.md): benchmark commands, charts,
89	  and CSV generation.
90	- [tests/test-vectors/README.md](tests/test-vectors/README.md): official
91	  continuation vectors used for regression checks.
92	
93	## Model Weights
94	
95	This implementation only works with the DeepSeek V4 Flash and PRO GGUFs published for
96	this project. It is not a general GGUF loader, and arbitrary DeepSeek/GGUF files
97	will not have the tensor layout, quantization mix, metadata, or optional MTP
98	state expected by the engine. The 2 bit quantizations provided here are not
99	a joke: they behave well, work under coding agents, call tools in a reliable way.
100	The 2 bit quants use a very asymmetrical quantization: only the routed MoE
101	experts are quantized, up/gate at `IQ2_XXS`, down at `Q2_K`. They are the
102	majority of all the model space: the other components (shared experts,
103	projections, routing) are left untouched to guarantee quality.
104	
105	Download one main model. **Prefer the imatrix versions.**
106	
107	```sh
108	./download_model.sh q2-imatrix   # 96/128 GB RAM machines, imatrix-tuned q2
109	./download_model.sh q2-q4-imatrix  # 96/128 GB RAM machines, q2 with last 6 layers q4
110	./download_model.sh q4-imatrix   # >= 256 GB RAM machines, imatrix-tuned q4
111	./download_model.sh pro-q2-imatrix  # 512 GB RAM machines, PRO q2 imatrix quant
112	```
113	
114	For the full PRO Q4 distributed run, download one half on each machine:
115	
116	```sh
117	./download_model.sh pro-q4-layers00-30      # first half of PRO Q4 split
118	./download_model.sh pro-q4-layers31-output  # second half of PRO Q4 split
119	```
120	
121	The script downloads from `https://huggingface.co/antirez/deepseek-v4-gguf`,
122	stores files under `./gguf/`, resumes partial downloads with `curl -C -`, and
123	updates `./ds4flash.gguf` to point at the selected main model.
124	The `pro-q4-layers00-30`, `pro-q4-layers31-output`, and `pro-q4-split` targets
125	download distributed PRO Q4 pieces and do not update `./ds4flash.gguf`.
126	Authentication is optional for public downloads, but `--token TOKEN`,
127	`HF_TOKEN`, or the local Hugging Face token cache are used when present.
128	
129	If you want to regenerate GGUF files or collect a new imatrix, see
130	[gguf-tools/README.md](gguf-tools/README.md). Those tools are meant for offline
131	model-building work and can take a long time on the full DeepSeek V4 Flash
132	weights. Flash GGUF generation is supported by the local tools. PRO GGUF
133	production currently still depends on the external `llama.cpp`-based workflow;
134	native tooling can be added later.
135	
136	`./download_model.sh mtp` fetches the optional speculative decoding support
137	GGUF for Flash. It can be used with q2-imatrix, q2-q4-imatrix, and q4-imatrix,
138	but must be enabled explicitly with `--mtp`. The current MTP/speculative
139	decoding path is still experimental: it is correctness-gated and currently
140	provides at most a slight speedup, not a meaningful generation-speed win.
141	
142	Then build:
143	
144	```sh
145	make                  # macOS Metal
146	make cuda-spark       # Linux CUDA, DGX Spark / GB10
147	make cuda-generic     # Linux CUDA, other local CUDA GPUs
148	make cpu              # CPU-only diagnostics build
149	```
150	
151	`./ds4flash.gguf` is the default model path used by both binaries. Pass `-m` to
152	select another supported GGUF from `./gguf/`. Run `./ds4 --help` and
153	`./ds4-server --help` for the full flag list.

180	## Running models larger than RAM
181	
182	The normal Metal path tries to make the model resident in GPU-addressable
183	memory. This is the fastest path and should remain your default when the model
184	fits. When it does not fit, DwarfStar also has a Metal-only **SSD streaming**
185	capacity mode. In this mode the non-routed model weights stay resident, while
186	routed MoE experts are kept in an in-memory cache and loaded from the GGUF file
187	on cache misses.
188	
189	Streaming is not as fast as fitting the full model in RAM. It still needs memory
190	for non-routed weights, KV cache, graph scratch, activations, and the routed
191	expert cache. It is useful because routed experts dominate model size and modern
192	Mac SSDs are fast enough to make cache misses tolerable. Long prefills can still
193	be fast; generation is more sensitive to cache misses because every new token
194	routes through experts again.
195	
196	Start with the automatic cache budget:
197	
198	```sh
199	./ds4 -m ./ds4flash.gguf --ssd-streaming
200	```
201	
202	If startup reports that the expert cache is too large, or if you want to reserve
203	more memory for context, set the routed expert cache explicitly:
204	
205	```sh
206	./ds4 -m ./ds4flash.gguf --ssd-streaming --ssd-streaming-cache-experts 32GB
207	```
208	
209	The `32GB` value is a memory budget for complete routed experts, not a generic
210	byte cache. DwarfStar converts it to the number of full experts that fit for the
211	current GGUF. Non-routed weights, KV cache, graph scratch, and activations need
212	additional memory. Only the automatic cache budget does the subtraction for you:
213	it takes 80% of the Metal recommended working set, subtracts non-routed weights,
214	then uses the rest for routed experts. Leave the hot expert preload enabled for
215	normal use; use `--ssd-streaming-cold` and `--ssd-streaming-preload-experts N`
216	only for measurements.
217	
218	### Practical SSD streaming examples
219	
220	On 64GB MacBooks, start with the 2-bit Flash GGUF and a moderate expert cache:
221	
222	```sh
223	./download_model.sh q2-imatrix
224	
225	./ds4 \
226	  -m ./ds4flash.gguf \
227	  --ssd-streaming \
228	  --ssd-streaming-cache-experts 32GB \
229	  --ctx 32768 \
230	  --nothink
231	```
232	
233	On 128GB MacBooks, PRO q2 streaming is experimental but usable for inspection
234	and occasional work when you accept slow generation. Start with `--nothink`:
235	
236	```sh
237	./download_model.sh pro-q2-imatrix
238	
239	./ds4 \
240	  -m gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-IQ2XXS-w2Q2K-AProjQ8-SExpQ8-OutQ8-Instruct-imatrix.gguf \
241	  --ssd-streaming \
242	  --ctx 32768 \
243	  --nothink
244	```
245	
246	On an M5 Max with 128GB of RAM, a short PRO q2 streaming decode benchmark found
247	the automatic budget best: it selected about `59GB` of routed expert cache.
248	Manual `64GB` to `75GB` caches were close on that machine. Larger explicit
249	`NGB` requests are capped before inference so the expert buffers remain
250	lockable instead of falling into macOS paging. If the system is under extra
251	memory pressure and `mlock` still fails, ds4 refuses to install pageable
252	expert-cache entries and releases a locked-cache margin before continuing with
253	the measured lockable cache size. Prefer the automatic budget; if setting the
254	cache manually on this class of machine, start around `48GB` to `64GB`, then
255	increase only while the startup log reports a lockable cache. Once the machine
256	is stable, re-enable thinking with a conservative generation limit:
257	
258	```sh
259	./ds4 \
260	  -m gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-IQ2XXS-w2Q2K-AProjQ8-SExpQ8-OutQ8-Instruct-imatrix.gguf \
261	  --ssd-streaming \
262	  --ctx 32768 \
263	  --think \
264	  --tokens 1500
265	```
266	
267	The important startup line is the cache report. Start conservative, then
268	increase the cache if the machine has headroom.
269	
270	## Distributed Inference
271	
272	Distributed inference lets DwarfStar **run a model that is too large for one machine** by
273	splitting transformer layers across multiple machines. The main example is the
274	full 4-bit Flash quant across two 128 GB MacBooks: each process maps only its
275	own layer slice, activations are sent over TCP, and the coordinator keeps normal
276	CLI/API behavior.
277	
278	Distributed inference also allows to **speed up prefill** by
279	using multiple GPUs at the same time to process different micro-batches at
280	different layers, like in an assembly line. Only prefill can be accelerated this
281	way. Generation is purely autoregressive: each token must finish across the
282	route before the next token can start. The model work is the same as a single
283	process, plus coordination latency, so distributed generation is slower.
284	
285	To build an initial mental model, here are the high level concepts:
286	
287	1. You put the GGUF on every machine, but each one loads just a subset. `--layers` controls which tensors are mapped, so a worker with `--layers 20:output` does not load the earlier layers.
288	2. Layer ranges are inclusive: `10:20` means layers 10, 11, ..., 20. `N:output` means layer `N` through the final layer plus the output head.
289	3. You assign one of the machines the role of `coordinator`, the others the roles of `workers`. Workers will connect to the coordinator and will tell they are there and which layers they are able to process.
290	4. Each worker keeps its slice of the KV cache.
291	5. Communication is worker-to-worker, there is no need to use the coordinator as relay, so if your coordinator is `A`, and you make a request, activations will flow in `A -> B -> C -> back to A`.
292	
293	### How it works and how to configure it
294	
295	The prefill path is pipelined (this is why it can go faster than in a single machine).
296	For large prompts the coordinator can run its
297	slice on chunk N+1 while the worker is running its slice on chunk N. The
298	distributed rows below were measured with two M5 Max 128 GB MacBooks connected
299	by Thunderbolt 5, using the Q4 Flash GGUF and the default 4096-token
300	distributed prefill chunk. The single-process column is a reference run with
301	the Q2 GGUF on a single machine, so it actually is a bit faster since
302	the routed MoEs are smaller.
303	
304	| Prompt | Single-process reference | Two MacBooks | Speedup |
305	| ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
306	| 9421 tokens | 421.70 t/s | 582.22 t/s | 1.38x |
307	| 28684 tokens | 405.30 t/s | 674.16 t/s | 1.66x |
308	| 63819 tokens | 353.62 t/s | 654.79 t/s | 1.85x |
309	
310	Generation is different. **It is strictly autoregressive**: token N+1 cannot start
311	until token N has produced logits and sampling has selected the next token. That
312	means distributed generation cannot use the long prefill pipeline. It pays at
313	least one cross-machine activation hop per generated token, so generation is
314	slower than a single local process. On the same two-Mac Thunderbolt setup, a
315	12k-context control run with the 91 GB Flash quant went from 30.59 t/s
316	single-process to 24.67 t/s distributed, a 19.4% loss. Distributed inference is
317	therefore mainly for fitting larger models and speeding up long prefills, not
318	for making decode faster.
319	
320	### Full DeepSeek V4 PRO Q4 on two Mac Studios
321	
322	The full-size PRO Q4 GGUF can be run across two 512 GB Mac Studio M3 Ultra
323	machines by giving the coordinator layers `0:30` and the worker
324	`31:output`. Use the split GGUF files so each side maps only the tensors it
325	needs:
326	
327	```sh
328	# Coordinator machine.
329	./download_model.sh pro-q4-layers00-30
330	
331	# Worker machine.
332	./download_model.sh pro-q4-layers31-output
333	```
334	
335	The two files are:
336	
337	```text
338	gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Q4K-Layers00-30.gguf
339	gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Q4K-Layers-31-output.gguf
340	```
341	
342	This is a capacity use case: each process maps only its own half of the model,
343	while the worker owns the output head and returns logits.
344	
345	The current PRO Q4 Metal path uses queue-resident exact expert tables for the
346	large routed experts. This avoids the broad multi-GiB routed-tensor bindings
347	that made early distributed PRO Q4 attempts either run very slowly or hit Metal
348	memory accounting limits. In a short greedy smoke test over the direct
349	`192.168.0.182` / `192.168.0.183` link, the model generated coherent text and
350	measured 11.47 t/s generation after startup. Per-token telemetry was balanced:
351	local layers were around 39-43 ms, remote layers around 44-49 ms, for total
352	token times around 84-92 ms. Expect a slow startup while each side maps and
353	makes its half of the model resident. Long-context PRO Q4 prefill and decode
354	performance still needs separate benchmarking.
355	
356	The measurements above use a Thunderbolt 5 cable. The implementation is plain
357	TCP and also works over slower links, including WiFi, but fast Ethernet or
358	Thunderbolt networking is strongly recommended. Slow links mostly hurt
359	generation latency and short prefills; large prefills can still benefit when
360	the layer split is balanced. In the normal performance path, the last worker
361	owns the output head and returns logits directly.
362	
363	Minimal two-host configuration:
364	
365	```sh
366	# Machine A: coordinator, owns tokenization, sampling, the prompt, and layers 0..30.
367	./ds4 \
368	  -m gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Q4K-Layers00-30.gguf \
369	  --role coordinator \
370	  --layers 0:30 \
371	  --listen 169.254.43.68 1234
372	
373	# Machine B: worker, connects to A and owns layers 31..output.
374	./ds4 \
375	  -m gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Q4K-Layers-31-output.gguf \
376	  --role worker \
377	  --layers 31:output \
378	  --coordinator 169.254.43.68 1234
379	```
380	
381	Normally the final worker should own the output head too, for example
382	`--layers 20:output`. This avoids returning a full final hidden-state batch
383	after prefill and lets the final worker produce the logits directly. On very
384	slow or metered links, `--layers 20:42` is also supported: the coordinator will
385	load the output head and compute logits locally, trading extra coordinator work
386	for smaller per-token replies.
387	
388	### Network Link Comparison
389	
390	The table below shows the same two M5 Max hosts, the same 91 GB Flash quant,
391	coordinator `--layers 0:19`, worker `--layers 20:output`, an 8192-token prompt
392	from `speed-bench/promessi_sposi.txt`, and 128 generated tokens. WiFi and
393	Internet numbers vary with local conditions, but the shape is the important
394	part: high latency hurts generation directly, while lower bandwidth also pulls
395	down long-prefill speed.
396	
397	| Link | Addresses | Ping avg | Prefill | Generation |
398	| --- | --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
399	| Thunderbolt 5 | `169.254.43.68` -> `169.254.12.245` | 0.45 ms | 582.99 t/s | 25.09 t/s |
400	| WiFi | `192.168.1.57` -> `192.168.1.95` | 77.20 ms | 250.70 t/s | 10.70 t/s |
401	| Internet / VPN | `10.77.0.4` -> `10.77.0.3` | 152.10 ms | 114.88 t/s | 3.63 t/s |
402	
403	The Internet/VPN case is not meant to be a good interactive experience. It is
404	still useful for collective testing: multiple people can temporarily combine
405	machines to run a larger model that would not fit on any single host, accepting
406	slow decode in exchange for being able to inspect the model at all.
407	
408	Use the coordinator exactly like normal `./ds4`: interactive chat, `/read`,
409	and ordinary generation go through the same high-level session API. The same
410	distributed options are also wired into `ds4-agent`, `ds4-eval`, and
411	`ds4-bench`. For benchmarks, workers should already be running; `ds4-bench`
412	waits until a complete route is available.
413	
414	Useful tuning and diagnostics:
415	
416	```sh
417	./ds4-bench \
418	  -m gguf/DeepSeek-V4-Flash-Q4KExperts-F16HC-F16Compressor-F16Indexer-Q8Attn-Q8Shared-Q8Out-chat-v2.gguf \
419	  --prompt-file speed-bench/promessi_sposi.txt \
420	  --ctx-start 32768 \
421	  --ctx-max 65536 \
422	  --step-incr 32768 \
423	  --gen-tokens 0 \
424	  --role coordinator \
425	  --layers 0:19 \
426	  --listen 169.254.43.68 1234 \
427	  --debug
428	```
429	
430	`--debug` on the coordinator prints route formation and per-hop telemetry:
431	layer range, token span, local evaluation time, downstream wait time, socket
432	send time, and input/output byte counts. This is the current profiling tool for
433	deciding whether a split is balanced. `--dist-prefill-window N` controls how
434	many prefill chunks may be in flight end-to-end; the default is conservative
435	and bounded. `--dist-prefill-chunk N` exists for experiments, but the default
436	4096-token chunk is the canonical setting and should be used unless you are
437	explicitly validating a different chunk size.
438	
439	By default DwarfStar sends hidden-state activations as 32-bit floats. To reduce
440	traffic, pass `--dist-activation-bits 16` or `--dist-activation-bits 8` on the
441	coordinator. This changes only the transport format between machines, not the
442	model weights or KV cache. 16-bit transport halves activation traffic and is the
443	first option to try on Ethernet or WiFi. 8-bit transport is more aggressive and
444	should be treated as an approximate/experimental mode unless you have validated
445	the output for your use case. However experimentally reduction activation
446	size didn't provide a significant improvement, so this option may be removed
447	in the future.
448	
449	**If a worker disconnects, the coordinator removes that worker from the active
450	route**. The request already in flight can fail, and later calls report an
451	incomplete route until a compatible worker reconnects and sends a new
452	registration. For live sessions, the coordinator keeps the token history and can
453	rebuild worker KV state by replaying the prefix when the route is available
454	again. Workers also validate a rolling 64-bit token-prefix hash on every work
455	item, so a restarted worker at position 0 cannot silently accept work for
456	position N; it reports the mismatch and the coordinator replays the current
457	transcript. Ctrl+C in the CLI and agent is cooperative: DwarfStar waits for the
458	current distributed token or prefill chunk to drain before returning control,
459	which avoids coordinator-caused KV splits. Saved agent/server sessions use the
460	same KV file format as single-machine sessions: during save the coordinator
461	fetches worker-owned layer tensors and serializes one normal payload; during
462	load it splits that payload over the currently registered route.
463	
464	### Distributed protocol overview
465	
466	At the protocol level there are two kinds of connections. Workers keep a
467	control TCP connection open to the coordinator and send a `HELLO` with their
468	model ID, model family, quant profile, layer slice, context capacity, and data
469	port. The coordinator uses these registrations to build a route that covers all
470	layers. Work then moves over low-latency TCP data connections: the coordinator
471	computes the first slice, sends a `WORK` frame with session ID, token positions,
472	rolling token-prefix hashes before and after the span, route information, and
473	hidden-state payload, and each worker computes its slice. Middle workers can
474	forward directly to the next worker. The final worker returns logits to the
475	coordinator, or ACKs for non-final prefill chunks so the prefill pipeline can
476	stay full. `RESULT` frames echo the request ID and the post-span hash. A worker
477	status error is handled differently from a socket failure: KV/hash mismatch can
478	be recovered by replaying the token history on the same route, while transport
479	failure drops the route and waits for a replacement worker. For persistent KV,
480	the coordinator opens worker data connections and sends snapshot save/load
481	messages for each worker-owned layer range; the disk payload remains a single
482	agent/server cache file. The protocol has no
483	encryption or authentication, and is not release-stable yet; coordinator and
484	workers should be built from the same commit and used on trusted machines and
485	trusted networks.
486	
487	## Reducing heat, power usage and fan noise
488	
489	Long local inference runs can keep the GPU busy for extended periods. If you
490	care more about heat, fan noise, battery life on MacBooks, or reducing thermal
491	stress on the hardware than about maximum throughput, use `--power N`.
492	
493	`--power 100` is the default and means full speed. Lower values ask DwarfStar to target
494	that percentage of GPU usage: `--power 70` targets about 70%, `--power 50`
495	targets about half usage, and so forth. DwarfStar does this by measuring GPU work time
496	and inserting small sleeps between work units: during prefill it sleeps between
497	layers, and during generation it sleeps between decoded tokens. This reduces
498	sustained load without changing model output.
499	
500	The option is available on the CLI, server, agent, eval, and benchmark tools,
501	for example:
502	
503	```sh
504	./ds4 --power 50
505	./ds4-agent --power 70
506	./ds4-server --power 40 --ctx 100000
507	```
508	
509	## Native agent
510	
511	DwarfStar features a native coding agent that works in a different way
512	than most other systems: the inference is controlled from within the agent
513	itself, without socket/API boundaries, so the session is represented
514	by the on-disk KV cache itself. Moreover the tools and the system prompt
515	are all designed vertically for DeepSeek v4 Flash and PRO. This provides a
516	few advantages:
517	
518	* Low latency experience, bounded mainly by the prefill speed limits. Displaying of generated text, tool calling, start of a new session are always instantaneous.
519	* Live progress bar during prefill time.
520	* No DSML tool calling conversion, the tools are handled natively in the LLM format.
521	* KV cache mismatch are impossible by construction, the current state is always the truth.
522	* Everything is tuned for this model.
523	* Ability to switch saved sessions with `/list` and `/switch`; full KV sessions resume without a prefill stage.
524	
525	Agent sessions are stored in `~/.ds4/kvcache`. Use `/save` to persist the
526	current session, `/list` to show saved sessions sorted by recent update time,
527	and `/switch <sha>` to resume one of them. The session ID is stable across
528	future saves and is derived from the first user prompt and creation time.
529	`/del <sha>` removes a saved session. `/strip <sha>` keeps the rendered
530	conversation text and title but removes the heavy KV payload; switching to a
531	stripped session rebuilds the KV cache by prefilling the saved text.
532	
533	Use `--chdir /path/to/ds4` when launching `ds4-agent` from another directory,
534	so relative runtime files such as `metal/*.metal` resolve from the project tree.
535	
536	However while the system already works, there is a lot of work to do
537	in order to make it ready for prime time. When finally the agent will reach
538	the wanted shape, we will *likely* split the server and the client creating a stateful
539	session-based protocol that can recreate all that in a client-server way.
540	
541	## Benchmarking
542	
543	`ds4-bench` measures instantaneous prefill and generation throughput at context
544	frontiers instead of reporting one whole-run average. It loads the model once,
545	walks a fixed token sequence to frontiers such as 2048, 4096, 6144, and uses
546	incremental prefill so each row measures only the newly-added token interval.
547	After each frontier it saves the live KV state to memory, generates a fixed
548	greedy non-EOS probe, restores the memory snapshot, and continues prefill.
549	
550	```sh
551	./ds4-bench \
552	  -m ds4flash.gguf \
553	  --prompt-file speed-bench/promessi_sposi.txt \
554	  --ctx-start 2048 \
555	  --ctx-max 65536 \
556	  --step-incr 2048 \
557	  --gen-tokens 128
558	```
559	
560	The example file is a cleaned public-domain Project Gutenberg text of
561	Alessandro Manzoni's *I Promessi Sposi* (ebook #45334), with the Gutenberg
562	header and footer removed: <https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/45334>.
563	
564	Use `--step-incr N` for different linear spacing, or `--step-mul F` for
565	exponential sweeps. Output is CSV with one row per frontier: latest prefill
566	interval tokens/sec, generation tokens/sec at that frontier, and
567	`kvcache_bytes`.
568	
569	Sessions prefill long prompts in 4096-token chunks by default. Set
570	`DS4_METAL_PREFILL_CHUNK=N` to compare another chunk size, for example `2048`
571	to match the strict official-vector checkpoint path, or
572	`DS4_METAL_PREFILL_CHUNK=0` to prefill a prompt as one whole batch when memory
573	allows. Changing the chunk changes the KV checkpoint/logit path, so compare it
574	as an explicit run configuration.
575	Chunked Metal prefill reuses the same range-capable layer-major graph for each
576	chunk, preserving absolute compressor/indexer boundaries while avoiding the old
577	per-layer chunk dispatch path.
578	
579	## Capability Evaluation
580	
581	`ds4-eval` is a small real-model integration benchmark. It is not a leaderboard
582	runner and should not be reported as an official GPQA, SuperGPQA, AIME, or
583	security benchmark score: the questions are an embedded 92-item subset chosen
584	to make local regression testing useful and visually inspectable. The program
585	loads the real GGUF, renders DeepSeek chat prompts, streams sampled tokens in a split-screen TUI, grades
586	the final answer, and prints a per-question report with prompt tokens,
587	generated tokens, pass/fail state, the model answer, and the correct answer.
588	
589	```sh
590	./ds4-eval -m ds4flash.gguf --trace /tmp/ds4-eval.txt
591	```
592	
593	The default run uses `--tokens 16000`, thinking mode enabled, and a soft/hard
594	`</think>` budget cutoff so the model has room to produce a visible answer.
595	`ds4-eval` sizes the context internally from the largest selected prompt plus
596	the generation budget, and refuses runs that would need more than 1M context
597	tokens. Press `p` to pause, `q` to exit and print the report, Up/Down to
598	inspect or select another question, and Enter to run the selected question next.
599	`--plain` disables the TUI.
600	
601	Use `--regrade-trace /path/to/trace.txt` to replay the current answer
602	extractor and scorer against a prior `--trace` file without loading the model
603	or regenerating tokens. This is useful when auditing evaluator changes: it
604	shows which cases changed, the old picked answer, the new picked answer, and a
605	pass/fail summary.
606	
607	For inference changes that can affect generation drift, keep this deterministic
608	q1..q4 token-count gate in the test plan:
609	
610	```sh
611	./ds4-eval \
612	  -m ds4flash.gguf \
613	  --plain \
614	  --questions 4 \
615	  --tokens 2048 \
616	  --temp 0 \
617	  --seed 1
618	```
619	
620	The generated-token counts must stay aligned with the baseline:
621	
622	| Question | Expected state | Expected generated tokens | Expected given/correct |
623	|---:|---|---:|---|
624	| 1 | `PASSED` | 2048 | `B` / `B` |
625	| 2 | `PASSED` | 438 | `C` / `C` |
626	| 3 | `PASSED` | 666 | `70` / `70` |
627	| 4 | `FAILED` | 2048 | `A` / `C` |
628	
629	The first 75 embedded questions are interleaved as 25 GPQA Diamond, 25 audited
630	SuperGPQA, and 25 AIME 2025 problems. The final 17 are an audited COMPSEC
631	subset of reduced single-function C/C++ vulnerability-localization questions.
632	The model is asked for the single best source line, or the smallest exact line
633	set only when the bug cannot be localized to one line; the scorer accepts small
634	audited ranges only when adjacent lines are equivalent locations for the same
635	bug. The order is
636	intentionally progressive: early questions are useful smoke tests, while later
637	questions are hard enough that a strong reasoning model should still miss some
638	of them. The SuperGPQA slice is curated rather than blind: upstream rows with
639	wrong keys, missing figures, or underspecified prompts are replaced with cleaner
640	rows.
641	
642	The set should be treated as a hard capability regression suite rather than
643	a pass/fail unit test.
644	
645	- **GPQA Diamond** contributes graduate-level science questions with
646	  multiple-choice answers. DeepSeek's model card reports strong results
647	  on full GPQA Diamond in thinking mode, but individual items still require
648	  careful physics, chemistry, or biology reasoning and are easy to lose with a
649	  small prompt/rendering or sampling regression.
650	- **SuperGPQA** contributes broad specialist knowledge and domain-transfer
651	  questions. The model-card SuperGPQA number is much lower than GPQA Diamond,
652	  so these items are expected to be uneven: some look mundane, others require
653	  niche professional knowledge or exact interpretation of a translated-style
654	  exam question.
655	- **AIME 2025** contributes exact-answer contest math. These are often the most
656	  unforgiving items in the set: no multiple-choice prior, no partial credit, and
657	  a single arithmetic or algebraic slip changes the grade.
658	- **COMPSEC** contributes single-function C/C++ security reasoning items
659	  reduced from public CVE writeups. These are not exploit prompts: the task is
660	  to identify the best source line where the defensive code flaw is introduced,
661	  or return `0` for a safe function.
662	
663	In practice this means `ds4-eval` should not be expected to produce a perfect
664	92/92 run. It is meant to answer a more useful engineering question: after a
665	kernel, quantization, prompt-rendering, KV-cache, or tool-streaming change, does
666	DeepSeek V4 Flash still solve a representative mix of hard science, broad
667	knowledge, exact math, and security-code problems while using the same inference
668	path users run?
669	
670	## CLI
671	
672	One-shot prompt:
673	
674	```sh
675	./ds4 -p "Explain Redis streams in one paragraph."
676	```
677	
678	No `-p` starts the interactive prompt:
679	
680	```sh
681	./ds4
682	ds4>
683	```
684	
685	The interactive CLI is a real multi-turn chat. It keeps the rendered chat
686	transcript and the live graph KV checkpoint, so each turn extends the previous
687	conversation. Useful commands are `/help`, `/think`, `/think-max`, `/nothink`,
688	`/ctx N`, `/read FILE`, and `/quit`. Ctrl+C interrupts the current generation
689	and returns to `ds4>`.
690	
691	The CLI defaults to thinking mode. Use `/nothink` or `--nothink` for direct
692	answers. `--mtp MTP.gguf --mtp-draft 2` enables the optional MTP speculative
693	path; it is useful only for greedy decoding, currently uses a confidence gate
694	(`--mtp-margin`) to avoid slow partial accepts, and should be treated as an
695	experimental slight-speedup path.
696	
697	## Server
698	
699	Start a local OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible server:
700	
701	```sh
702	./ds4-server --ctx 100000 --kv-disk-dir /tmp/ds4-kv --kv-disk-space-mb 8192
703	```
704	
705	Use `--chdir /path/to/ds4` when launching `ds4-server` from another directory,
706	so relative runtime files such as `metal/*.metal` resolve from the project tree.
707	
708	The server keeps one mutable backend/KV checkpoint in memory,
709	so stateless clients that resend a longer version of the same prompt can reuse
710	the shared prefix instead of pre-filling from token zero.
711	
712	Request parsing and sockets run in client threads, but inference itself is
713	serialized through one graph worker. The current server does not batch multiple
714	independent requests together; concurrent requests wait their turn on the single
715	live graph/session.
716	
717	Supported endpoints:
718	
719	- `GET /v1/models`
720	- `GET /v1/models/deepseek-v4-flash`
721	- `GET /v1/models/deepseek-v4-pro`
722	- `POST /v1/chat/completions`
723	- `POST /v1/responses`
724	- `POST /v1/completions`
725	- `POST /v1/messages`
726	
727	The Flash and PRO model endpoints are compatibility aliases. They both report
728	the model currently loaded from the GGUF passed with `-m`; the endpoint name does
729	not select a different model.
730	
731	`/v1/chat/completions` accepts the usual OpenAI-style `messages`,
732	`max_tokens`/`max_completion_tokens`, `temperature`, `top_p`, `top_k`, `min_p`,
733	`seed`, `stream`, `stream_options.include_usage`, `tools`, and `tool_choice`.
734	Tool schemas are rendered into DeepSeek's DSML tool format, and generated DSML
735	tool calls are mapped back to OpenAI tool calls.
736	
737	`/v1/responses` accepts OpenAI Responses-style `input`, `instructions`,
738	`tools`, `tool_choice`, `max_output_tokens`, `temperature`, `top_p`, `stream`,
739	and `reasoning`. It is the preferred endpoint for Codex CLI. The server keeps
740	Responses continuations bound to live state when possible, and can fall back to
741	the same DSML rendering and KV prefix reuse used by chat completions.
742	
743	`/v1/messages` is the Anthropic-compatible endpoint used by Claude Code style
744	clients. It accepts `system`, `messages`, `tools`, `tool_choice`, `max_tokens`,
745	`temperature`, `top_p`, `top_k`, `stream`, `stop_sequences`, and thinking
746	controls. Tool uses are returned as Anthropic `tool_use` blocks.
747	
748	Default sampled API generation uses `temperature=1`, `top_p=1`, and
749	`min_p=0.05`, so the default filter is relative probability rather than
750	nucleus mass. In thinking mode DwarfStar uses those fixed sampling defaults and
751	ignores client sampling knobs, matching DeepSeek's fixed-thinking API behavior.
752	
753	The chat, Responses, and Anthropic endpoints support SSE streaming. In thinking
754	mode, reasoning is streamed in the native API shape instead of being mixed into
755	final text. OpenAI chat streaming
756	also streams tool calls as soon as the DSML invocation is recognized: the tool
757	header is sent first, then parameter bytes are forwarded as
758	`tool_calls[].function.arguments` deltas while generation continues. The
759	Anthropic endpoint streams thinking and text live, then emits structured
760	`tool_use` blocks when the generated tool block is complete.
761	The Responses endpoint streams the Responses event lifecycle expected by Codex,
762	including `response.output_text.delta`, function-call argument events, and
763	terminal `response.completed` / `response.incomplete` / `response.failed`
764	events.
765	
766	For browser JavaScript clients served from another origin, start the server with
767	`--cors` to emit `Access-Control-Allow-*` headers. This only changes HTTP
768	headers; it does not expose the server on the LAN. Use `--host 0.0.0.0`
769	explicitly when remote machines should be able to connect.
770	
771	### Tool call handling and canonicalization
772	
773	DeepSeek V4 emits tool calls as [DSML text](https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro/blob/main/encoding/README.md). Agent clients do not send that
774	same text back on the next request: they send normalized OpenAI/Anthropic JSON
775	tool-call objects. **If the server re-rendered those objects slightly
776	differently, the rendered byte prefix would no longer match the live KV
777	checkpoint** and the next turn would have to be rebuilt.
778	
779	The first line of defense is exact replay. Every tool call gets an unguessable
780	API tool ID, and the server remembers `tool id -> exact sampled DSML block` in
781	a bounded in-memory map backed by radix trees. When the client later sends that
782	tool ID back, the prompt renderer uses the exact DSML bytes the model sampled,
783	not a freshly formatted approximation. This map can also be saved inside KV
784	cache files, so exact replay survives server restarts for cached histories.
785	
786	**Canonicalization is only the backup path**. If the exact DSML block is missing,
787	or exact replay is disabled with `--disable-exact-dsml-tool-replay`, the server
788	renders a deterministic DSML form from the JSON tool object. After a tool-call
789	turn, it compares the live sampled token stream with the prompt that the next
790	client request will render. If needed, it rewrites the live checkpoint, or
791	falls back to an older disk KV snapshot and replays only the suffix. This keeps
792	the model continuation aligned with the stateless API transcript.
793	
794	During generation, the server also treats DSML syntax differently from payload.
795	When the model is emitting stable protocol structure such as DSML tags,
796	parameter headers, JSON punctuation, or closing markers, sampling is forced to
797	`temperature=0` so the tool call stays parseable. This greedy mode does **not**
798	apply to argument payloads: `string=true` parameter bodies and JSON string
799	values, including file contents and edit text, use the request's normal sampling
800	settings. That separation is important: deterministic decoding is helpful for
801	syntax, but can create repeated text when applied to long code or file bodies.
802	
803	Minimal OpenAI example:
804	
805	```sh
806	curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/chat/completions \
807	  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
808	  -d '{
809	    "model":"deepseek-v4-flash",
810	    "messages":[{"role":"user","content":"List three Redis design principles."}],
811	    "stream":true
812	  }'
813	```
814	
815	### Agent Client Usage
816	
817	`ds4-server` can be used by local coding agents that speak OpenAI-compatible
818	chat completions. Start the server first, and set the client context limit no
819	higher than the `--ctx` value you started the server with:
820	
821	```sh
822	./ds4-server --ctx 100000 --kv-disk-dir /tmp/ds4-kv --kv-disk-space-mb 8192
823	```
824	
825	You can use larger context and larger cache if you wish. Full context of
826	1M tokens is going to use more or less 26GB of memory (compressed indexer
827	alone will be like 22GB), so configure a context which makes sense in
828	your system. With 128GB of RAM you would run the 2-bit quants, which are
829	already 81GB, 26GB are going to be likely too much, so a context window
830	of 100~300k tokens is wiser. However users reported being able to run 2bit
831	quants with 250k ctx window in a Macs with just 96GB of system memory: make sure
832	to kill processes that use too much memory, if you plan doing so ;)
833	
834	The `384000` output limit below avoids token caps since the model is able
835	to generate very long replies otherwise (up to 384k tokens). The server
836	still stops when the configured context window is full.
837	
838	For **opencode**, add a provider and agent entry to
839	`~/.config/opencode/opencode.json`:
840	
841	```json
842	{
843	  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
844	  "provider": {
845	    "ds4": {
846	      "name": "ds4.c (local)",
847	      "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
848	      "options": {
849	        "baseURL": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1",
850	        "apiKey": "dsv4-local"
851	      },
852	      "models": {
853	        "deepseek-v4-flash": {
854	          "name": "DeepSeek V4 Flash (ds4.c local)",
855	          "limit": {
856	            "context": 100000,
857	            "output": 384000
858	          }
859	        }
860	      }
861	    }
862	  },
863	  "agent": {
864	    "ds4": {
865	      "description": "DeepSeek V4 Flash served by local ds4-server",
866	      "model": "ds4/deepseek-v4-flash",
867	      "temperature": 0
868	    }
869	  }
870	}
871	```
872	
873	For **Pi**, add a provider to `~/.pi/agent/models.json`:
874	
875	```json
876	{
877	  "providers": {
878	    "ds4": {
879	      "name": "ds4.c local",
880	      "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1",
881	      "api": "openai-completions",
882	      "apiKey": "dsv4-local",
883	      "compat": {
884	        "supportsStore": false,
885	        "supportsDeveloperRole": false,
886	        "supportsReasoningEffort": true,
887	        "supportsUsageInStreaming": true,
888	        "maxTokensField": "max_tokens",
889	        "supportsStrictMode": false,
890	        "thinkingFormat": "deepseek",
891	        "requiresReasoningContentOnAssistantMessages": true
892	      },
893	      "models": [
894	        {
895	          "id": "deepseek-v4-flash",
896	          "name": "DeepSeek V4 Flash (ds4.c local)",
897	          "reasoning": true,
898	          "thinkingLevelMap": {
899	            "off": null,
900	            "minimal": "low",
901	            "low": "low",
902	            "medium": "medium",
903	            "high": "high",
904	            "xhigh": "xhigh"
905	          },
906	          "input": ["text"],
907	          "contextWindow": 100000,
908	          "maxTokens": 384000,
909	          "cost": {
910	            "input": 0,
911	            "output": 0,
912	            "cacheRead": 0,
913	            "cacheWrite": 0
914	          }
915	        }
916	      ]
917	    }
918	  }
919	}
920	```
921	
922	Optionally make it the default Pi model in `~/.pi/agent/settings.json`:
923	
924	```json
925	{
926	  "defaultProvider": "ds4",
927	  "defaultModel": "deepseek-v4-flash"
928	}
929	```
930	
931	For **Codex CLI**, use the Responses wire API:
932	
933	```toml
934	[model_providers.ds4]
935	name = "DS4"
936	base_url = "http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1"
937	wire_api = "responses"
938	stream_idle_timeout_ms = 1000000
939	```
940	
941	Then run:
942	
943	```sh
944	codex --model deepseek-v4-flash -c model_provider=ds4
945	```
946	
947	For **Claude Code**, use the Anthropic-compatible endpoint. A wrapper like this
948	matches the local `~/bin/claude-ds4` setup:
949	
950	```sh
951	#!/bin/sh
952	unset ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
953	
954	export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="${DS4_ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL:-http://127.0.0.1:8000}"
955	export ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN="${DS4_API_KEY:-dsv4-local}"
956	export ANTHROPIC_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
957	
958	export ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_MODEL_OPTION="deepseek-v4-flash"
959	export ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_MODEL_OPTION_NAME="DeepSeek V4 Flash local ds4"
960	export ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_MODEL_OPTION_DESCRIPTION="ds4.c local GGUF"
961	
962	export ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
963	export ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
964	export ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
965	export CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
966	
967	export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC=1
968	export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONSTREAMING_FALLBACK=1
969	export CLAUDE_STREAM_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS=600000
970	
971	exec "$HOME/.local/bin/claude" "$@"
972	```
973	
974	Claude Code may send a large initial prompt, often around 25k tokens, before it
975	starts doing useful work. Keep `--kv-disk-dir` enabled: after the first expensive
976	prefill, the disk KV cache lets later continuations or restarted sessions reuse
977	the saved prefix instead of processing the whole prompt again.
978	
979	## Thinking Modes
980	
981	DeepSeek V4 Flash has distinct non-thinking, thinking, and Think Max modes.
982	The server defaults to thinking mode. `reasoning_effort=max` requests Think
983	Max, but it is only applied when the context size is large enough for the model
984	card recommendation; smaller contexts fall back to normal thinking. OpenAI
985	`reasoning_effort=xhigh` still maps to normal thinking, not Think Max.
986	
987	For direct replies, use `thinking: {"type":"disabled"}`, `think:false`, or a
988	non-thinking model alias such as `deepseek-chat`.
989	
990	## Disk KV Cache
991	
992	Chat/completion APIs are stateless: agent clients usually resend the whole
993	conversation every request. `ds4-server` first tries the cheap exact token-prefix
994	check, then falls back to comparing rendered prompt bytes with decoded
995	checkpoint bytes. The live in-memory checkpoint covers the current session; the
996	disk KV cache makes useful prefixes survive session switches and server
997	restarts.
998	
999	For RAM reasons there is currently only one live KV cache in memory. When a new
1000	unrelated session replaces it, the old checkpoint can only be resumed without
1001	re-processing if it was written to the disk KV cache. In other words, memory
1002	cache handles the active session; disk cache is the resume mechanism for
1003	different sessions.
1004	
1005	Enable it with:
1006	
1007	```sh
1008	./ds4-server --kv-disk-dir /tmp/ds4-kv --kv-disk-space-mb 8192
1009	```
1010	
1011	The cache key is the SHA1 of the rendered byte prefix, and files are named
1012	`<sha1>.kv`. The DS4 payload still stores the exact token IDs and graph state
1013	for that prefix. This matters for continued chats: the model may have generated
1014	one token whose decoded text is later sent back by a client as two canonical
1015	prompt tokens. A rendered byte-prefix hit can still reuse the checkpoint and
1016	tokenize only the new suffix.
1017	The file is intentionally written with ordinary `read`/`write` I/O, not
1018	`mmap`, so restoring cache entries does not add more VM mappings to a process
1019	that already maps the model.
1020	
1021	Tool calls also keep a bounded exact-DSML replay map keyed by unguessable tool
1022	IDs, so client JSON history can be rendered back to the exact sampled text. The
1023	RAM map keeps up to 100000 IDs by default; tune it with `--tool-memory-max-ids`.
1024	Use `--disable-exact-dsml-tool-replay` to disable this and fall back to
1025	canonical JSON-to-DSML rendering.
1026	
1027	On disk, a cache file is:
1028	
1029	```text
1030	KVC fixed header, 48 bytes
1031	u32 rendered_text_bytes
1032	rendered_text_bytes of UTF-8-ish token text
1033	DS4 session payload, payload_bytes from the KVC header
1034	optional tool-id map section
1035	```
1036	
1037	The fixed header is little-endian:
1038	
1039	```text
1040	0   u8[3]  magic = "KVC"
1041	3   u8     version = 1
1042	4   u8     routed expert quant bits, currently 2 or 4
1043	5   u8     save reason: 0 unknown, 1 cold, 2 continued, 3 evict, 4 shutdown
1044	6   u8     extension flags, bit 0 = appended tool-id map
1045	7   u8     reserved
1046	8   u32    cached token count
1047	12  u32    hit count
1048	16  u32    context size the snapshot was written for
1049	20  u8[4]  reserved
1050	24  u64    creation Unix time
1051	32  u64    last-used Unix time
1052	40  u64    DS4 session payload byte count
1053	```
1054	
1055	The rendered text is the tokenizer-decoded text for the cached token prefix.
1056	It is both the human-inspectable prefix and the lookup identity: its SHA1 is
1057	the filename, and a file is reusable only when those bytes are a prefix of the
1058	incoming rendered prompt. After load, the exact checkpoint tokens from the DS4
1059	payload remain authoritative, and only the incoming text suffix after the cached
1060	bytes is tokenized.
1061	
1062	The optional tool-id map is present only when header extension bit 0 is set.
1063	Appended sections use fixed bit order, so future extension bits can add fields
1064	without ambiguity. The map stores unguessable API tool call IDs back to the
1065	exact DSML block the model sampled. Only mappings whose DSML block is present
1066	in the rendered cached text are stored. This lets restarted servers render
1067	later client history byte-for-byte like the original model output, even if the
1068	client reorders JSON arguments.
1069	
1070	The current tool-id map section is:
1071	
1072	```text
1073	0   u8[3]  magic = "KTM"
1074	3   u8     version = 1
1075	4   u32    entry count
1076	
1077	For each entry:
1078	0   u32    tool id byte length
1079	4   u32    sampled DSML byte length
1080	8   bytes  tool id
1081	... bytes  exact sampled DSML block
1082	```
1083	
1084	The section is auxiliary replay memory, not model state. A cache hit restores
1085	the session payload first, then loads the map if present. Before rendering a
1086	request, the server can also scan cache files for the tool IDs present in the
1087	client history and load just those mappings, so an exact DSML replay can survive
1088	server restarts even when the matching KV snapshot is not the one ultimately
1089	used for the rendered-prefix hit.
1090	
1091	The DS4 session payload starts with thirteen little-endian `u32` fields:
1092	
1093	```text
1094	0   magic = "DSV4"
1095	1   payload version = 2
1096	2   saved context size
1097	3   prefill chunk size
1098	4   raw KV ring capacity
1099	5   raw sliding-window length
1100	6   compressed KV capacity
1101	7   checkpoint token count
1102	8   layer count
1103	9   raw/head KV dimension
1104	10  indexer head dimension
1105	11  vocabulary size
1106	12  live raw rows serialized below
1107	```
1108	
1109	Then it stores:
1110	
1111	- `u32[token_count]` checkpoint token IDs.
1112	- `float32[vocab_size]` logits for the next token after that checkpoint.
1113	- `u32[layer_count]` compressed attention row counts.
1114	- `u32[layer_count]` ratio-4 indexer row counts.
1115	- For every layer: the live raw sliding-window KV rows, written in logical
1116	  position order rather than physical ring order.
1117	- For compressed layers: live compressed KV rows and compressor frontier
1118	  tensors.
1119	- For ratio-4 compressed layers: live indexer compressed rows and indexer
1120	  frontier tensors.
1121	
1122	The logits are raw IEEE-754 `float32` values from the host `ds4_session`
1123	buffer. They are saved immediately after the checkpoint tokens so a loaded
1124	snapshot can sample or continue from the exact next-token distribution without
1125	running one extra decode step. MTP draft logits/state are not persisted; after
1126	loading a disk checkpoint the draft state is invalidated and rebuilt by normal
1127	generation.
1128	
1129	Distributed coordinator sessions use the same `DSV4` payload. Worker-owned
1130	layer tensors are pulled during save and merged into the normal layer-ordered
1131	tensor stream; during load the coordinator splits that stream into the current
1132	route and pushes the relevant layer tensors back to the workers. The saved file
1133	does not retain the distributed topology.
1134	
1135	The tensor payload is DS4-specific KV/session state, not a generic inference
1136	graph dump. It is expected to be portable only across compatible `ds4.c`
1137	builds for this model layout.
1138	
1139	The cache stores checkpoints at four moments:
1140	
1141	- `cold`: after a long first prompt reaches a stable prefix, before generation.
1142	- `continued`: when prefill or generation reaches the next absolute aligned frontier.
1143	- `evict`: before an unrelated request replaces the live in-memory session.
1144	- `shutdown`: when the server exits cleanly.
1145	
1146	Cold saves intentionally trim a small token suffix and align down to a prefill
1147	chunk boundary. This avoids common BPE boundary retokenization misses when a
1148	future request appends text to the same prompt. The defaults are conservative:
1149	store prefixes of at least 512 tokens, cold-save prompts up to 30000 tokens,
1150	trim 32 tail tokens, and align to 2048-token chunks. The important knobs are:
1151	
1152	Continued saves use the same alignment and are written only when the live graph
1153	naturally reaches an absolute frontier. With the defaults this means roughly
1154	every 10k tokens, independent of where the first cold checkpoint landed, so long
1155	generations leave restart points behind without persisting the fragile final few
1156	tokens.
1157	
1158	- `--kv-cache-min-tokens`
1159	- `--kv-cache-cold-max-tokens`
1160	- `--kv-cache-continued-interval-tokens`
1161	- `--kv-cache-boundary-trim-tokens`
1162	- `--kv-cache-boundary-align-tokens`
1163	- `--tool-memory-max-ids`
1164	- `--disable-exact-dsml-tool-replay`
1165	
1166	By default, checkpoints may be reused across the 2-bit and 4-bit routed-expert
1167	variants if the rendered prefix matches. Use `--kv-cache-reject-different-quant`
1168	when you want strict same-quant reuse only.
1169	
1170	The cache directory is disposable. If behavior looks suspicious, stop the
1171	server and remove it. You can investigate what is cached with hexdump as
1172	the kv cache files include the verbatim prompt cached.
1173	
1174	## Backends
1175	
1176	The default graph backend is Metal on macOS and CUDA in CUDA builds:
1177	
1178	```sh
1179	./ds4 -p "Hello" --metal
1180	./ds4 -p "Hello" --cuda
1181	```
1182	
1183	On Linux, plain `make` prints the available build targets instead of selecting a
1184	CUDA target implicitly. Use `make cuda-spark` for DGX Spark / GB10. It omits an
1185	explicit `nvcc -arch` because that is currently the fastest path on GB10. Use
1186	`make cuda-generic` for a normal local CUDA build, or set `CUDA_ARCH` explicitly
1187	when cross-building or when you need a known target:
1188	
1189	```sh
1190	make cuda CUDA_ARCH=sm_120
1191	make cuda CUDA_ARCH=native
1192	```
1193	
1194	There is also a CPU reference/debug path:
1195	
1196	```sh
1197	./ds4 -p "Hello" --cpu
1198	make cpu
1199	./ds4
1200	./ds4 -p "Hello"
1201	```
1202	
1203	Do not treat the CPU path as the production target. The CLI and `ds4-server`
1204	support the CPU backend for reference/debug use and share the same KV session
1205	and snapshot format as Metal and CUDA, but normal inference should use Metal or
1206	CUDA.
1207	
1208	## Steering
1209	
1210	This project supports steering with single-vector activation directions; see the
1211	`dir-steering` directory for more information. This follows the core idea of the
1212	[Refusal in Language Models Is Mediated by a Single Direction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.11717)
1213	paper. You can use it to make the model more or less verbose, less likely to
1214	answer programming questions if it is a chatbot for your car rental web site,
1215	and so forth, much faster than fine-tuning.
1216	This is also useful for cybersecurity researchers who want to reduce a model's
1217	willingness to provide dual-use or offensive security guidance.
1218	
1219	## Test Vectors
1220	
1221	`tests/test-vectors` contains short and long-context continuation vectors
1222	captured from the official DeepSeek V4 Flash API. The requests use
1223	`deepseek-v4-flash`, greedy decoding, thinking disabled, and the maximum
1224	`top_logprobs` slice exposed by the API. Local vectors are generated with
1225	`./ds4 --dump-logprobs` and compared by token bytes, so tokenizer/template or
1226	attention regressions show up before they become long generation failures. The
1227	C runner pins `DS4_METAL_PREFILL_CHUNK=2048` for this strict API-vector
1228	comparison.
1229	
1230	All project tests are driven by the C runner, with a small `ds4-eval`
1231	extractor self-test run first:
1232	
1233	```sh
1234	make test                  # ./ds4-eval --self-test-extractors && ./ds4_test --all
1235	./ds4_test --logprob-vectors
1236	./ds4_test --server
1237	```
1238	
1239	## Debugging Notes
1240	
1241	When a generation looks wrong, three small tools are usually enough to get a
1242	first answer:
1243	
1244	```sh
1245	./ds4 --dump-tokens -p "..."
1246	./ds4 --dump-logprobs /tmp/out.json --logprobs-top-k 20 --temp 0 -p "..."
1247	./ds4 --dump-logits /tmp/logits.json --metal --nothink --prompt-file prompt.txt
1248	./ds4-server --trace /tmp/ds4-trace.txt ...
1249	```
1250	
1251	- `--dump-tokens` tokenizes the `-p` or `--prompt-file` string exactly as
1252	  written, recognizes DS4 protocol specials, and then exits before inference
1253	  starts. For example, the DSML tool close marker starts as two tokens: `</`
1254	  and `｜DSML｜`.
1255	- `--dump-logprobs` stores a greedy continuation with the top local
1256	  alternatives at each step, which helps separate sampling choices from
1257	  logit/model issues.
1258	- `ds4-server --trace` writes the rendered prompts, cache decisions, generated
1259	  text, and tool-parser events for a whole agent session.
1260

file.content:
# DwarfStar

**DwarfStar** is a small native inference engine optimized first for
**DeepSeek V4 Flash**, with support for **DeepSeek V4 PRO** on very high-memory
machines. It is
intentionally narrow: not a generic GGUF runner, not a wrapper around another
runtime: it is completely self-contained. Other than running the model in a
correct and fast way, the project goal is to provide DeepSeek specific loading,
prompt rendering, tool calling, KV state handling (RAM and on-disk), server
API and integrated coding agent, all ready to work with coding agents or with
the provided CLI interface. There are also tools for GGUF and imatrix generation,
and for quality and speed testing.

We support the following backends:
* **Metal** is our primary target. Starting from MacBooks with 96GB of RAM (or less, using SSD streaming).
* **NVIDIA CUDA / DGX Spark**, CUDA with special care for the DGX Spark.
* **Strix Halo (ROCm)**, systems like the Framework Desktop and other systems based on the same GPU and unified RAM design.

This project would not exist without **llama.cpp and GGML**, make sure to read
the acknowledgements section, a big thank you to Georgi Gerganov and all the
other contributors.

**Note that DeepSeek v4** is not our only target. Right now Flash and PRO are the
perfect fit because of capabilities, size, KV cache efficiency. If tomorrow a
better open weight model is released for the 128GB size, we could switch, the same
for other important size classes like 512GB of RAM. The project is stictly
opportunistic depending on what open weight models exist in a given moment.
If a new model will be supported, the old one may be removed completely and
no longer supported, unless there is some kind of overlap of abilities.

## Motivations

* Very capable open weight models finally exist. DeepSeek v4 Flash feels quasi-frontier. The PRO is even better. Both resist 2 bit quantization very well.
* Very capable computers like MacBooks, the DGX Spark now exist.
* DeepSeek v4 kv cache design makes it pratical to run very big contexts. Other vendors are using this approach.
* This few hundred billions models are strictly better than smaller (even if dense) models, regardless of what benchmarks say.

That said, a few important things about this project:

* The local inference landscape contains many excellent projects, but new models are released continuously, and the attention immediately gets captured by the next model to implement. This project takes a deliberately narrow bet: one model at a time, official-vector validation (logits obtained with the official implementation), long-context tests, and enough agent integration to know if it really works. The exact model may change as the landscape evolves, but the constraint remains: local inference credible on high end personal machines or Mac Studios, starting from 96/128GB of memory.
* This software is developed with **strong assistance from GPT 5.5** and with humans leading the ideas, testing, and debugging. We say this openly because it shaped how the project was built. If you are not happy with AI-developed code, this software is not for you. The acknowledgement below is equally important: this would not exist without `llama.cpp` and GGML, largely written by hand.
* This implementation is based on the idea that compressed KV caches like the one of DeepSeek v4 and the fast SSD disks of modern MacBooks should change our idea that KV cache belongs to RAM. **The KV cache is actually a first-class disk citizen**. Fast SSD disks also changed the inference game from the point of view of "model needs to fit RAM": while having more RAM the the model size is still preferred, SSD streaming allows to turn the available amount of RAM from a hard cutoff (can I run this model or not?) to continuous spectrum of speed levels.
* Our vision is that local inference should be a set of three things working well together, out of the box: A) inference engine with HTTP API + B) GGUF specially crafted to run well under a given engine and given assumptions + C) testing and validation with coding agents implementations. D) Purpose built agents for specific models and execution environments. DwarfStar only runs with the GGUF files provided. It gets tested against officially obtained logits at different context sizes. This project exists because we wanted to make one local model feel finished end to end, not just runnable. However this is beta quality code, so probably we are not still there, especially since recently we introduced large new features: distributed inference, SSD streaming, and other minor improvements.
* The optimized graph path targets **Metal on macOS** and **CUDA on Linux**. The CPU path is only for correctness checks and model/tokenizer diagnostics. For CPU-only Linux builds, use `make cpu`; it builds the normal `./ds4` and `./ds4-server` binaries without CUDA or Metal. On macOS, **warning: current macOS versions have a bug in the virtual memory implementation that will crash the kernel** if you try to run the CPU code. Remember? Software sucks. It was not possible to fix the CPU inference to avoid crashing, since each time you have to restart the computer, which is not funny. Help us, if you have the guts.

## Acknowledgements to llama.cpp and GGML

`ds4.c` does not link against GGML, but it **exists thanks to the path opened by the
llama.cpp project and the kernels, quantization formats, GGUF ecosystem, and hard-won
engineering knowledge developed there**.
We are thankful and indebted to [`llama.cpp`](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp)
and its contributors. Their implementation, kernels, tests, and design choices were
an essential reference while building this DeepSeek V4 specific inference path.
Some source-level pieces are retained or adapted here under the MIT license: GGUF
quant layouts and tables, CPU quant/dot logic, and certain kernels. For this
reason, and because we are genuinely grateful, we keep the GGML authors copyright
notice in our `LICENSE` file.

## Status

The code and GGUF files are to be considered of **beta quality** because
inference and model serving is a complicated matter and all this exists
only for a few days. It will take months to reach a more stable form.
However, we try to keep the project in a usable state, and we are making
progress. If you have issues, make sure to use `--trace` to log the
sessions, and open issues including the full trace.

The `ds4-agent` is alpha quality, the project was later added.

## More Documentation

If you are looking for very specific things, we have other
sub-README files. Otherwise for normal usage keep reading the
next sections.

- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md): correctness and speed regression testing
  guide for contributors. **Read this before sending a pull request**.
- [gguf-tools/README.md](gguf-tools/README.md): offline GGUF generation,
  imatrix collection, quantization tooling, and quality checks.
- [gguf-tools/imatrix/README.md](gguf-tools/imatrix/README.md): how the
  routed-MoE imatrix is collected and used.
- [gguf-tools/imatrix/dataset/README.md](gguf-tools/imatrix/dataset/README.md):
  how the calibration prompt corpus is generated.
- [gguf-tools/quality-testing/README.md](gguf-tools/quality-testing/README.md):
  how local GGUFs are scored against official DeepSeek V4 Flash/PRO continuations.
- [dir-steering/README.md](dir-steering/README.md): directional steering data,
  vector generation, and usage.
- [speed-bench/README.md](speed-bench/README.md): benchmark commands, charts,
  and CSV generation.
- [tests/test-vectors/README.md](tests/test-vectors/README.md): official
  continuation vectors used for regression checks.

## Model Weights

This implementation only works with the DeepSeek V4 Flash and PRO GGUFs published for
this project. It is not a general GGUF loader, and arbitrary DeepSeek/GGUF files
will not have the tensor layout, quantization mix, metadata, or optional MTP
state expected by the engine. The 2 bit quantizations provided here are not
a joke: they behave well, work under coding agents, call tools in a reliable way.
The 2 bit quants use a very asymmetrical quantization: only the routed MoE
experts are quantized, up/gate at `IQ2_XXS`, down at `Q2_K`. They are the
majority of all the model space: the other components (shared experts,
projections, routing) are left untouched to guarantee quality.

Download one main model. **Prefer the imatrix versions.**

```sh
./download_model.sh q2-imatrix   # 96/128 GB RAM machines, imatrix-tuned q2
./download_model.sh q2-q4-imatrix  # 96/128 GB RAM machines, q2 with last 6 layers q4
./download_model.sh q4-imatrix   # >= 256 GB RAM machines, imatrix-tuned q4
./download_model.sh pro-q2-imatrix  # 512 GB RAM machines, PRO q2 imatrix quant
```

For the full PRO Q4 distributed run, download one half on each machine:

```sh
./download_model.sh pro-q4-layers00-30      # first half of PRO Q4 split
./download_model.sh pro-q4-layers31-output  # second half of PRO Q4 split
```

The script downloads from `https://huggingface.co/antirez/deepseek-v4-gguf`,
stores files under `./gguf/`, resumes partial downloads with `curl -C -`, and
updates `./ds4flash.gguf` to point at the selected main model.
The `pro-q4-layers00-30`, `pro-q4-layers31-output`, and `pro-q4-split` targets
download distributed PRO Q4 pieces and do not update `./ds4flash.gguf`.
Authentication is optional for public downloads, but `--token TOKEN`,
`HF_TOKEN`, or the local Hugging Face token cache are used when present.
